Thesis_finding: Panel rationalisation intensity is Pareto-active. This means panel standardisation creates genuine cost-carbon-time trade-offs through repetition benefits, transport/lifting effects, and material/handling penalties.
Practitioner_action: Use branch summary, TOPSIS, or stakeholder weighting to choose the preferred panel rationalisation level.

Thesis_finding: Truss rationalisation intensity is Pareto-active. This means truss span-family standardisation creates trade-offs between repetition benefits and possible span mismatch, self-weight, transport, and detailing penalties.
Practitioner_action: Compare truss rationalisation using branch summary and stakeholder ranking.

Thesis_finding: Panel rationalisation strategy creates genuine cost-carbon-time branches. The preferred panel scheme depends on project priorities.
Practitioner_action: Select panel scheme using TOPSIS/stakeholder weighting or branch summary.

Thesis_finding: One truss scheme dominates the Pareto front. If this is scheme 0, aggressive truss span reduction does not provide enough benefit to offset grouping/oversizing/setup penalties for this building.
Practitioner_action: Fix truss_scheme at the Pareto-selected value.

Thesis_finding: Opening/detailing complexity creates objective trade-offs. The preferred level depends on cost, carbon, and time priorities.
Practitioner_action: Treat opening/detailing complexity as a stakeholder-dependent detailing decision.

Thesis_finding: Prefabrication level is Pareto-active. It drives the cost-carbon-time trade-off: higher prefabrication can reduce time and site-stage carbon, but may increase factory/logistics cost beyond the optimal range.
Practitioner_action: Use TOPSIS, stakeholder weighting, or robustness ranking to select P_f.
